name: Bhai Gian Singh Surjit
body: Bhai Gian Singh Surjit was born on 2nd August 1940 into the home of Sardar Banta Singh Ji in the village of Moranwali in Hoshiarpur district of Punjab. He lost his eyesight at a very young age as a side effect of some medication that was given to him. At the age of 10, he was sent to Daudhar, Moga to learn music. Subsequently, he became a student of the renowned Prof. Darshan Singh Komal, who was one of the custodians of Bhai Sain Ditta Ji’s legacy. Some of the other notable students of Prof. Darshan Singh Komal include Bhai Didar Singh, Bhai Dharam Singh Zakhmi, Bhai Chanan Singh Majboor, Bhai Beant Singh Bijli and many others.

Bhai Gian Singh Surjit moved to the United Kingdom in 1968 and based himself in Gravesend, Kent. On the 5th of October 1969, he got married to Sardarni Karamjit Kaur in Kent. Soon after arriving in the UK, Bhai Gian Singh Surjit formed a ‘jatha’ with Bhai Gurbachan Singh Hamdard and they made a name for themselves in those early years. Bhai Gian Singh Surjit was adept in singing many Raags set to difficult Taals and used to delight the listeners with his performances during various Raag Darbars held throughout the UK. Besides performing Kirtan, he was also a wonderful teacher who touched the lives of many through his love for teaching. Another contribution in the field of music was his collaboration with Bhai Sital Singh Sitara in 2002 to publish a book on Gurmat Sangeet for the future generations to learn from. This book was dedicated to their teacher, Prof. Darshan Singh Komal, and it was titled ‘Gurbani Komal Sangeet’.

In his later years, his attention was more focused on charity work. Along with three of his friends who were also visually impaired, Bhai Gian Singh Surjit set up the Guru Nanak Missionary Blind Society in Britain. The other three individuals involved in this initiative were Bhai Sital Singh Sitara, Bhai Rajinder Singh Bhatti and Bhai Avtar Singh Mann. Subsequently, they also established the Guru Nanak Mission Netarheen Birdh Ashram in Phagwara which has been operational for almost three decades since 1993. This Ashram serves as a charity centre for the blind, orphans, elderly and physically disabled. They provide vocational training in Braille, music, painting, sewing and various other useful skills. At the moment, there are between 150-200 children at the Ashram and every year about 15 to 20 of them get employed after graduation. All four of them worked tirelessly to raise funds for this Ashram and to ensure its success.

Bhai Gian Singh Surjit was a diabetic and also had to cope with hypertension. In 1992, he had his first heart attack and in 1993, he underwent a triple bypass procedure. He also suffered a stroke in 1999. Despite his health issues, he remained in good spirits and continued his charity work, performing and teaching Kirtan. Bhai Gian Singh Surjit took his last breath on 20th October 2006 and left for his heavenly abode. He is left behind by his wife of 37 years, Sardarni Karamjit Kaur, his three children: Jasbir Kaur (Jassay), Ranbir Singh (Raja) and Harjot Singh (Joti) and his grandchildren, Jasmin, Amrik, Aaron, Arjun and Taran.

The information above was obtained from the book ‘Punjab De Parsidh Raag Rababi’ by Sardar Balbir Singh Kanwal and also the ‘Sikhi Wiki’ page on Bhai Gian Singh Surjit. If anyone has more recordings of Bhai Gian Singh Surjit to share, please contact us.
